summary: American poetic movement of the 1950s-60s focused on personal experience, emotional trauma, mental illness, and sexuality. Term coined by M.L. Rosenthal in a review of Robert Lowell's Life Studies. Key figures include Robert Lowell, Sylvia Plath, Anne Sexton, John Berryman, and W.D. Snodgrass.

works.name: The Movement
works.description: Confessional poetry emerged in the late 1950s, characterized by direct, colloquial style and autobiographical content about personal trauma.

clues.clue: Term coined by M.L. Rosenthal in a review of Robert Lowell's Life Studies

focuses on individual experience and personal trauma

works.name: Key Works: Life Studies (Robert Lowell)
works.description: Lowell's landmark 1959 collection containing "Skunk Hour," "Beyond the Alps," and the prose memoir "91 Revere Street."

clues.clue: Life Studies reviewed by M.L. Rosenthal, coining the term — contains "Skunk Hour" (skunk jabs wedge-head in sour cream)
examples: critics coined in reviews of the collection Life Studies, which features a poem titled for an animal who 'jabs her wedge-head in a cup of sour cream'
Its name was coined in a review of Robert Lowell's Life Studies

clues.clue: "Beyond the Alps" — train journey from Rome to Paris, discusses dwindling Catholic faith (first poem in Life Studies)
examples: A train journey from Rome to Paris prompts a discussion of dwindling Catholic faith in the first poem of a collection from this movement

clues.clue: "Skunk Hour" — describes "where the graveyard shelves on the town" and "red fox stain" covering Blue Hill; narrator states "I myself am Hell"
examples: describes 'where the graveyard shelves on the town' and a 'red fox stain' that covers 'Blue Hill'
A radio bleats 'Love, O careless Love...' and the narrator states that, 'I myself am Hell'

works.name: Key Works: Heart's Needle (W.D. Snodgrass)
works.description: Snodgrass's 1960 Pulitzer-winning collection. Ten-poem sequence about his divorce and separation from his daughter Cynthia.

clues.clue: Heart's Needle — title and epigraph from The Madness of Suibhne; about separation from daughter Cynthia; interspersed with Korean War imagery
examples: inspired by the author's separation from his daughter, Cynthia Jean
dedicated to 'Cynthia' and is interspersed with imagery from the Korean War

clues.clue: Won 1960 Pulitzer Prize — one of earliest/most influential confessional works
examples: won its author the 1960 Pulitzer Prize for Poetry
one of the most influential works in this movement

clues.clue: "Arguably launched" the movement — remark about "an only daughter" from The Madness of Suibhne
examples: This movement was arguably launched by a poem titled for a remark about 'an only daughter' in The Madness of Suibhne

works.name: Key Works: Ariel (Sylvia Plath)
works.description: Plath's posthumous collection edited by Ted Hughes, named for her horse. Contains "Lady Lazarus" and "Daddy."

clues.clue: "Lady Lazarus" — declares "White Godiva, I unpeel" after opening "Stasis in darkness"; ends with "the red eye, the cauldron of morning"
examples: a poem that declares, 'White Godiva, I unpeel' after opening, 'Stasis in darkness.' That poem, which ends with the image of 'the red eye, the cauldron of morning'

works.name: Key Works: Anne Sexton
works.description: Sexton's collections To Bedlam and Part Way Back and The Awful Rowing Toward God.

clues.clue: "Her Kind" — opens "I have gone out, a possessed witch" (from To Bedlam and Part Way Back)

comprehensive_summary:
Confessional poetry is an American poetic movement of the 1950s and 1960s characterized by its focus on personal experience, emotional trauma, mental illness, and sexuality, using a direct and colloquial style. The term was coined by critic M.L. Rosenthal in a review of Robert Lowell's Life Studies.

The movement was arguably launched by W.D. Snodgrass's Heart's Needle, a ten-poem sequence about his divorce and separation from his daughter Cynthia, with its title and epigraph drawn from The Madness of Suibhne. The collection, which alternates personal imagery with scenes from the Korean War, won the 1960 Pulitzer Prize.

Robert Lowell's Life Studies (1959) is the collection whose review gave the movement its name. It contains "Beyond the Alps" (a train journey from Rome to Paris discussing dwindling Catholic faith), "91 Revere Street" (a prose memoir), and "Skunk Hour" (describing "where the graveyard shelves on the town," a "red fox stain" covering Blue Hill, and the narrator's declaration "I myself am Hell").

Sylvia Plath's Ariel, edited posthumously by Ted Hughes and named for her horse, contains poems like "Lady Lazarus" ("White Godiva, I unpeel" / "the red eye, the cauldron of morning") and "Daddy." Anne Sexton's To Bedlam and Part Way Back includes "Her Kind" ("I have gone out, a possessed witch") and "Wanting to Die" (suicides "want to know which tools. They never ask why build"). Her collection The Awful Rowing Toward God ends with the speaker getting a royal flush in poker upon reaching "the island called God."

John Berryman's Dream Songs, featuring alter ego Henry (sometimes appearing in blackface, addressed as Mr. Bones), are often grouped with confessional poetry. Delmore Schwartz's epic Genesis was an early precursor. Other figures associated with the movement include Sharon Olds.
